name: Icelandic Sheepdog
description: The Icelandic Sheepdog is Iceland's only native dog breed, descended directly from the Nordic Spitz dogs brought to Iceland by the Viking settlers who colonized the island around 874 AD, making it one of the purest ancient breeds in existence. Iceland's geographic isolation preserved the genetic purity of the breed for over a thousand years until distemper nearly wiped them out in the 20th century, before conservation efforts by the Icelandic Sheepdog Association and global breeders stabilized the population. They are energetic, cheerful, and characteristically vocal dogs used traditionally to herd and protect sheep and cattle in Iceland's rugged volcanic terrain.

conditions_detail.name: Hip Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Hip dysplasia is a genetic condition where the hip joint doesn't develop properly, causing the ball and socket to not fit together correctly. This leads to joint deterioration, pain, and arthritis over time.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Treatment ranges from weight management and physical therapy for mild cases to total hip replacement for severe cases. NSAIDs and joint supplements help manage pain.
prevention_tips: Screen breeding stock with hip scoring

conditions_detail.name: Patellar Luxation
conditions_detail.description: Patellar luxation occurs when the kneecap slips out of its normal groove in the femur. It can be medial (inward) or lateral (outward) and ranges from grade 1 (occasional slipping) to grade 4 (permanent dislocation).
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Grade 1-2 cases are managed conservatively with weight control and physiotherapy. Grade 3-4 typically require surgical correction to deepen the patellar groove and realign the tibial crest.
prevention_tips: Breed from dogs with normal patellar scores
Avoid high-impact activities in affected puppies

conditions_detail.name: Malocclusion
conditions_detail.description: Malocclusion is abnormal tooth alignment due to jaw length discrepancy or individual tooth malposition. It ranges from breed-specific normal variation (brachycephalic underbite) to clinically significant abnormalities causing tooth-on-tooth or tooth-on-palate trauma.
symptoms: Lower teeth hitting upper palate (causing ulcers)
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Minor malocclusions are monitored. Clinically significant cases causing soft tissue trauma require orthodontic treatment (bite plates, crown reduction) or extraction of offending teeth.
prevention_tips: Do not breed dogs with clinically significant inherited malocclusion
Monitor deciduous dentition and extract retained baby teeth promptly
